verdict.visual_pleasure: Newsreader serif on a warm cream paper at a disciplined ~34rem measure reads beautifully; drop caps on openers, centered pull-quotes set off by hairline rules, italic anchor blockquotes, and a Tufte sidenote gutter give real editorial rhythm. clamp()-based fluid type, pretty-wrapped paragraphs, and a clean three-state theme make every chapter inviting. I kept wanting to scroll. The one density caveat — chapters are very heavily built from verbatim anchor blockquotes — actually works here because the source prose is gripping, but it does make some chapters read as quote-after-quote rather than synthesized narrative.
verdict.navigation: Genuinely serves reading. Header pips mark all 22 sections and jump on click; the summoned overlay TOC opens with 't', closes with Esc, and supports arrow-key navigation with focus management; a within-chapter scroll progress bar tracks position; 'Continue to Chapter N' buttons and a prev button anchor each chapter end; xref popovers, deep-link hashes, and persisted theme round it out. One chapter visible at a time keeps cognitive load low. Minor: the 'Continue to Chapter N' label is computed from the current index so a couple of transitions (e.g. prologue→ch1) read slightly off by one, but it never misroutes.
verdict.comprehension: answered 10/10. Every question was fully answerable from the HTML alone and I scored each correct: trauma's physiological changes (prologue), amygdala-as-smoke-detector with hippocampal feedback (ch4), MPFC watchtower vs amygdala in PTSD (ch4), left/right explicit-implicit memory with the dedicated comparison table (ch3), ACE study with far more than three findings (ch9), secure vs disorganized 'fright without solution' attachment (ch7), EMDR bilateral stimulation and the revisit-without-retraumatization mechanism (ch15), yoga restoring HRV/interoception (ch16), neurofeedback right-temporal first study and alpha training (ch19), and theater's shared foundation tied to IFS self-leadership (ch17/ch20). 10/10 = 5.
verdict.illustration: The 20 diagrams genuinely externalize relationships rather than decorate. Mermaid flowcharts carry real logic — the learned-helplessness reenactment loop, the bottom-up three-tier brain, the thalamus→amygdala/MPFC fast-vs-slow road, Porges's descending autonomic ladder, the interoception→agency self-sensing loop, the EMDR sequence diagram, the breath→SNS/PNS→HRV chain, and the IFS Self/managers/firefighters/exiles tree. The hand-built inline SVG window-of-tolerance (hyper/window/hypo bands) is a clean conceptual map. Comparison tables do heavy lifting: left/right brain, top-down vs bottom-up, Stan vs Ute, four attachment patterns with prevalences, two kinds of memory, brain-wave bands, three theater programs. They map onto exactly the relationships the questions probe.
verdict.books_finished_rate: Yes, decisively. At ~56,600 words this is roughly a third of the 460-page original, chaptered so each sitting is a clean 10-15 minute unit with a visible finish line — the opposite of the original's daunting bulk. I answered all 10 comprehension questions correctly from this alone, which tells me it covers the working-knowledge surface (neurobiology, attachment, ACE, the full treatment menu) faithfully. I would skip the original and trust this for ~80% of working-knowledge purposes, and it would measurably raise my books-finished rate. It earns 5 rather than just clearing the bar: the chaptering and per-chapter progress make a long condensation feel light.
